title: Non-game

infobox.value: | [Development](/wiki/Video_game_development "Video game development")  * [Producer](/wiki/Video_game_producer "Video game producer") * [Developer](/wiki/Video_game_developer "Video game developer")    + [Designer](/wiki/Video_game_design#Game_designer "Video game design")   + [Artist](/wiki/Game_art_design#Video_game_artist "Game art design")   + [Programmer](/wiki/Video_game_programmer "Video game programmer")  ---  * [Design](/wiki/Video_game_design "Video game design")   + [Level design](/wiki/Level_(video_games)#Level_design "Level (video games)") * [Programming](/wiki/Video_game_programming "Video game programming")   + [Engine](/wiki/Game_engine "Game engine")   + [AI](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence_in_video_games "Artificial intelligence in video games")   + [Creation system](/wiki/Game_creation_system "Game creation system") * [Graphics](/wiki/Video_game_graphics "Video game graphics") * [Music](/wiki/Video_game_music "Video game music") * [Testing](/wiki/Game_testing "Game testing")

| * [v](/wiki/Template:Art_and_video_games "Template:Art and video games") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Art_and_video_games "Template talk:Art and video games") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Art_and_video_games "Special:EditPage/Template:Art and video games")

are a class of [software](/wiki/Software "Software") on the border between [video games](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") and [toys](/wiki/Toy "Toy"). The term "non-game game" was coined by late [Nintendo](/wiki/Nintendo "Nintendo") president [Satoru Iwata](/wiki/Satoru_Iwata "Satoru Iwata"), who describes it as "a form of entertainment that really doesn't have a winner, or even a real conclusion".[[1]](#cite_note-1) [Will Wright](/wiki/Will_Wright_(game_designer) "Will Wright (game designer)") had previously used the term "software toy" for the same purpose.[[2]](#cite_note-book-interview-2): 11, 412 The main difference between non-games and traditional video games is the lack of structured goals, objectives, and challenges.[[3]](#cite_note-3) This allows the player a greater degree of self-expression through freeform play, since they can set up their own goals to achieve. Some genres that have been considered non-games include [language-learning software](/wiki/Computer-assisted_language_learning "Computer-assisted language learning"), [digital tabletop games](/wiki/Digital_tabletop_game "Digital tabletop game"), [simulation video games](/wiki/Simulation_video_game "Simulation video game"), and [art games](/wiki/Art_game "Art game").

**Non-games** are a class of [software](/wiki/Software "Software") on the border between [video games](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") and [toys](/wiki/Toy "Toy"). The term "non-game game" was coined by late [Nintendo](/wiki/Nintendo "Nintendo") president [Satoru Iwata](/wiki/Satoru_Iwata "Satoru Iwata"), who describes it as "a form of entertainment that really doesn't have a winner, or even a real conclusion".[[1]](#cite_note-1) [Will Wright](/wiki/Will_Wright_(game_designer) "Will Wright (game designer)") had previously used the term "software toy" for the same purpose.[[2]](#cite_note-book-interview-2): 11, 412 The main difference between non-games and traditional video games is the lack of structured goals, objectives, and challenges.[[3]](#cite_note-3) This allows the player a greater degree of self-expression through freeform play, since they can set up their own goals to achieve. Some genres that have been considered non-games include [language-learning software](/wiki/Computer-assisted_language_learning "Computer-assisted language learning"), [digital tabletop games](/wiki/Digital_tabletop_game "Digital tabletop game"), [simulation video games](/wiki/Simulation_video_game "Simulation video game"), and [art games](/wiki/Art_game "Art game").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Non-game&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Non-games have existed since the early days of video games, although there hasn't been a specific term for them. One of the first is Atari Inc.’s 1977 *[Surround](/wiki/Surround_(video_game) "Surround (video game)")*, a two-player [snake game](/wiki/Snake_(video_game_genre) "Snake (video game genre)") for the [Atari VCS](/wiki/Atari_2600 "Atari 2600"), which contains a free-form drawing mode called "Video Graffiti." Later examples which were sold as games but present a less structured experience are *[Alien Garden](/wiki/Alien_Garden "Alien Garden")* (Epyx, 1982), *[Moondust](/wiki/Moondust_(video_game) "Moondust (video game)")* (Creative Software, 1983), *[Worms?](/wiki/Worms%3F "Worms?")* (one of the 1983 launch titles from Electronic Arts), *[I, Robot](/wiki/I,_Robot_(video_game) "I, Robot (video game)")* (Atari, 1983) which contains an "ungame mode" called "Doodle City," and [Jeff Minter's](/wiki/Jeff_Minter "Jeff Minter") *[Psychedelia](/wiki/Psychedelia_(light_synthesizer) "Psychedelia (light synthesizer)")* (Llamasoft, 1984), which is an interactive light synthesizer.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/ACS_rashid01.png/250px-ACS_rashid01.png)](/wiki/File:ACS_rashid01.png)

A map created in *Adventure Construction Set*

[Bill Budge](/wiki/Bill_Budge "Bill Budge")'s *[Pinball Construction Set](/wiki/Pinball_Construction_Set "Pinball Construction Set")* (Electronic Arts, 1983) popularized software where building something is more entertaining than playing the finished product. To a lesser extent, some games became construction sets through the inclusion of level editors, like [Doug Smith](/wiki/Douglas_E._Smith "Douglas E. Smith")'s *[Lode Runner](/wiki/Lode_Runner "Lode Runner")* (Broderbund, 1983), Ron Rosen's *[Mr. Robot and His Robot Factory](/wiki/Mr._Robot_and_His_Robot_Factory "Mr. Robot and His Robot Factory")* (Datamost, 1983), and John Anderson's *[Rally Speedway](/wiki/Rally_Speedway "Rally Speedway")* (Adventure International, 1983). Other more proper construction sets followed, such as EA's *[Adventure Construction Set](/wiki/Adventure_Construction_Set "Adventure Construction Set")* (1984) and *[Racing Destruction Set](/wiki/Racing_Destruction_Set "Racing Destruction Set")* (1985).

In January 1984, [Joel Gluck](/w/index.php?title=Joel_Gluck&action=edit&redlink=1 "Joel Gluck (page does not exist)") presented a simple toy called *Bounce* in his game design column in *[ANALOG Computing](/wiki/ANALOG_Computing "ANALOG Computing")*.[[4]](#cite_note-4) *Bounce* lets users draw low-resolution lines, then release a block that leaves a trail as it moves across the screen, making patterns as it reflects off of obstacles (other than its own trail). The program is specifically designed not to have goals or scorekeeping, other than what's in the user's head. *Bounce* was revisited several times in *ANALOG*, including a version which allows multiple active blocks at once.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The 1989 simulation game *[SimCity](/wiki/SimCity "SimCity")* was called a *software toy* by its creator [Will Wright](/wiki/Will_Wright_(game_designer) "Will Wright (game designer)"), since there is no ultimate objective in the main game;[[2]](#cite_note-book-interview-2): 413 scenarios with objectives exist in some incarnations of the game, such as *[SimCity 2000](/wiki/SimCity_2000 "SimCity 2000")*, but these are not the focus.

Non-games have been particularly successful on the [Nintendo DS](/wiki/Nintendo_DS "Nintendo DS") and [Wii](/wiki/Wii "Wii"), where a broad range of Japanese titles have appealed to a growing number of [casual gamers](/wiki/Casual_gamer "Casual gamer").[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-IGN-7) Some examples of non-game genres are: [gamified](/wiki/Gamification_of_learning "Gamification of learning") [language-learning software](/wiki/Computer-assisted_language_learning "Computer-assisted language learning") for English and Japanese (including one for the memorization of [kanji](/wiki/Kanji "Kanji")), [Go](/wiki/Go_(game) "Go (game)")-learning games, [puzzle](/wiki/Puzzle_video_game "Puzzle video game") (e. g., [Tetris](/wiki/Tetris "Tetris")), [sandbox](/wiki/Sandbox_game "Sandbox game") (such as [Minecraft](/wiki/Minecraft "Minecraft")), as well as [simulation](/wiki/Simulation_game "Simulation game"), [art](/wiki/Art_game "Art game"), and [cooking](/wiki/Cooking_game "Cooking game") games.[[7]](#cite_note-IGN-7)
